name: Zig ZIR and AIR
summary: Zig's two-stage frontend IR: ZIR (untyped, comptime-evaluable) is generated from the AST and feeds semantic analysis. AIR (analyzed) is what ZIR becomes after types are resolved, comptime evaluated, generic functions instantiated, and dead branches removed.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Zig has a two-IR frontend. **ZIR** (Zig Intermediate Representation) is generated from the AST and fed to semantic analysis. The defining design choice: ZIR encodes *unanalyzed* code — types are not yet resolved, comptime evaluation has not yet run, generic instantiation has not happened. Each ZIR instruction stores `node_offset` relative to its parent declaration, so source-position metadata is compact and survives source-buffer freeing.

**AIR** is what ZIR becomes after semantic analysis: types resolved, comptime evaluated, generic functions instantiated, dead branches removed. AIR is lower-level than ZIR — closer to a typed CFG with explicit operands — and is the input to Zig's LLVM backend (or its self-hosted backends targeting WebAssembly, x86_64, or aarch64).

sections.title: Why two stages exist
sections.content:
The two-IR architecture exists because comptime evaluation needs to operate on un-analyzed code (ZIR) while codegen needs analyzed code (AIR). A single IR could carry both, but at the cost of either making comptime see analyzed code (impossible, since comptime drives instantiation) or making codegen consume un-analyzed code (impractical for an LLVM backend that needs concrete types).

ZIR is also the substrate for Zig's `comptime`: comptime evaluation is interpretation of ZIR with the analyzer choosing instantiation values. Once analysis completes, ZIR is lowered to AIR.

sections.title: Lowering and self-hosted backends
sections.content:
AIR feeds either LLVM (the historical primary backend) or Zig's own self-hosted backends targeting x86_64, aarch64, RISC-V, and WebAssembly. The self-hosted backends are what allow Zig to bootstrap without LLVM in some configurations and to ship faster compile times for development builds.

The two-stage design also makes incremental compilation more tractable: ZIR for a function only needs to be regenerated when the AST changes, and AIR only when types change. This is part of how Zig hopes to deliver fast incremental rebuilds at scale.
